coredata.dc:title: Dual-mechanism detecting fluoride and tetracycline in food matrices using red-emitting carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Hao, Yumin
Wen, Xiaole
Zheng, Xiaofang
Wang, Huiping
Dong, Wenjuan
Liu, Yang
Song, Shengmei
Shuang, Shaomin
Guo, Yujing
Dong, Chuan
Gong, Xiaojuan
coredata.dc:description: Excessive exposure to fluoride and tetracycline can cause severe dental damage, including tetracycline-induced tooth discoloration and dental fluorosis. Herein, we introduce a dual-mechanism sensing strategy using red-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs) for the independent detection of fluoride and tetracycline. A key advantage of R-CDs as sensors is their ability to selectively identify both analytes through long-wavelength emission with a large Stokes shift. For fluoride detection, we developed a fluorescence-enhanced sensor based on R-CDs-Fe3+ via a competitive binding mechanism. Meanwhile, tetracycline detection was achieved using a fluorescence-quenching sensor leveraging static quenching and the internal filter effect (IFE). The successful quantification of fluoride and tetracycline in food matrices demonstrates the practical potential of R-CDs in food safety monitoring. Additionally, this study presents a novel framework for designing multi-target detection systems using a single type of carbon dots across different sensing mechanisms.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluoride
Tetracycline
Dual-mechanism sensing
Carbon dots
Large Stokes shift

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of F− by R-CDs-Fe3+-based fluorescence enhanced sensor
Detection of TC using the R-CDs-based fluorescence quenching sensor
Detection of F− and TC in actual samples
Optical properties and stability of R-CDs
Construction and optical mechanism of the fluorescence-enhanced sensor
R-CDs-Fe3+-based fluorescence-enhanced sensor for F− detection
Fluorescence quenching and recovery mechanism for F− detection
Quenching fluorescence sensor for TC detection
Fluorescence response of R-CDs to TC
Quenching mechanism of R-CDs by TC

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Central Guidance Fund for Science and Technology Development (YDZJSX2021A004 and YDZJSX2022C002), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21705101 and 22274090), and Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province (202403021212049).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis process of R-CDs. (b) TEM image, high-resolution TEM image (top-right inset), and diameter distribution (bottom-right inset) of R-CDs. (c) FTIR spectrum and (d) XPS survey scan of R-CDs. Deconvoluted XPS spectra of (e) carbon (C 1s), (f) nitrogen (N 1s), and (g) oxygen (O 1s) for R-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption (blue) and excitation/emission (red) spectra of R-CDs. (b) Two-dimensional excitation-emission mapping of R-CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescence quenching of R-CDs (600 nm emission) by Fe3+ under excitation wavelengths of 285 nm and 505 nm, with fluorescence recovery upon F− addition. (b) FL spectra of R-CDs. (c) FL spectra before (black) and after (green) Fe3+ addition. (d) FL spectra before (green) and after (pink) F− addition. (e) FL spectra of the R-CDs-Fe3+ system (CR-CDs = 0.01 mg•mL−1, CFe 3+ = 100 μM) after adding various concentrations of F− (0.5–120 μM). (f) Plot of the FL intensity of R-CDs versus F− concentration (0.5–120 μM). (g) FL intensity of the R-CDs-Fe3+ system with various anions (1.0 mM, red bars) and subsequent addition of F− (120 μM, green bars). All fluorescence measurements were taken under excitation wavelengths of 285 nm and 505 nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Linear relationship between 1/(F0–F) and 1/CFe 3+ (CR-CDs = 0.01 mg•mL−1). (b) Schematic diagram of the interaction between Fe3+, R-CDs, and F−. (c) TEM and HRTEM images of the R-CDs-Fe3+ complex. (d) TEM and HRTEM images of the R-CDs-Fe3+ complex after fluorescence recovery with F−.
XPS spectra of the R-CDs-Fe3+ complex before and after fluorescence recovery with F−: (a, b) C 1s, (c, d) N 1s, (e, f) O 1s, (g) F 1s, and (h, i) Fe 2p.

(a) FL spectra of R-CDs (0.01 mg•mL−1) upon adding various concentrations of TC (0.5–300 μM). F0 and F are FL intensities of R-CDs before and after TC addition, respectively. ΔF denotes the difference between F0 and F. (b) Plot of the FL intensity ratio (Fλex=285 nm/Fλex=505 nm) versus TC concentration (0.5–300 μM). Inset: Linear relationship in the range of 0.5–130 μM. (c) FL intensity ratio of R-CDs after adding various drugs (1.0 mM, red bars) and subsequent addition of TC (300 μM, green bars). 0–27: see Experimental Section. (d) Cyclic voltammetry curve of R-CDs. (e) Band gap calculation from the UV–vis absorption spectrum of R-CDs (CR-CDs = 0.02 mg•mL−1). (f) Electron transfer mechanism between R-CDs and TC. (g) Overlapping excitation spectrum (red line) of R-CDs and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of TC (blue line). (h) Observed (E obsd ) and corrected (E corr ) fluorescence suppression efficiency (E = 1-F/F0) of R-CDs (0.01 mg•mL−1) after TC addition. ΔE represents the difference between E obsd and E corr . (i) Fluorescence lifetime of R-CDs in the absence and presence of TC. (j) Stern-Volmer plots of F0/F versus TC concentration at different temperatures. (k) Lineweaver-Burk plots of 1/(F0–F) versus 1/CTC at various temperatures. (l) Schematic diagram of the ratiometric detection of TC. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of the synthesis and application of R-CDs for dual-mechanism detection of F − and TC.
Determination results of F− in toothpaste and tea samples (n = 6).
Applications of R-CDs in water and food samples for TC detection (n = 6).

head.ce:title: Dual-mechanism detecting fluoride and tetracycline in food matrices using red-emitting carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering and Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering and Institute of Environmental Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering and Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Excessive exposure to fluoride and tetracycline can cause severe dental damage, including tetracycline-induced tooth discoloration and dental fluorosis. Herein, we introduce a dual-mechanism sensing strategy using red-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs) for the independent detection of fluoride and tetracycline. A key advantage of R-CDs as sensors is their ability to selectively identify both analytes through long-wavelength emission with a large Stokes shift. For fluoride detection, we developed a fluorescence-enhanced sensor based on R-CDs-Fe3+ via a competitive binding mechanism. Meanwhile, tetracycline detection was achieved using a fluorescence-quenching sensor leveraging static quenching and the internal filter effect (IFE). The successful quantification of fluoride and tetracycline in food matrices demonstrates the practical potential of R-CDs in food safety monitoring. Additionally, this study presents a novel framework for designing multi-target detection systems using a single type of carbon dots across different sensing mechanisms.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Rapid industrialization and excessive consumption have led to the accumulation of fluoride (F−) and tetracycline (TC) in the environment, ultimately contaminating water and food sources [1,2]. Excessive intake of these substances poses serious risks to both ecosystems and human health, including skeletal damage and dental disorders [3,4]. Consequently, the accurate and efficient detection of F− and TC is critical for food safety, water quality assessment, and environmental monitoring [5,6]. Fluorescence-based detection has gained significant attention due to its simplicity, rapid response, and high selectivity [7,8]. Various fluorescent materials, including organic molecules, metal-organic frameworks, and semiconductor quantum dots, have been explored for F− and TC sensing [9–14]. However, many of these materials suffer from drawbacks such as complex synthesis, low solubility, high toxicity, and poor optical stability [15,16], limiting their practical applicability. Therefore, developing alternative materials that overcome these limitations is essential. In this regard, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted significant attention.

As a rapidly emerging class of zero-dimensional fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, CDs have been widely applied in sensing and imaging, particularly in medical diagnostics, due to their simple synthesis, high solubility, excellent biocompatibility, and tunable optical properties [17]. Extensive research has explored CDs-analyte interactions, establishing key sensing mechanisms such as the inner filter effect (IFE), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), and photo-induced electron transfer (PET) [18]. These mechanisms enable fluorescence quenching or enhancement, providing valuable tools for analyte detection. Additionally, CDs with high fluorescence quantum yield, long-wavelength emission, and large Stokes shift help minimize interference from biological autofluorescence, improving sensitivity in analytical applications [19,20]. Long-wavelength emissive CDs, in particular, have emerged as promising candidates for food safety and environmental monitoring. Several long-wavelength emissive CDs have been developed for F− detection [21–24]. However, most suffer from a small Stokes shift, which limits their sensitivity. Moreover, while numerous fluorescence sensors have been designed for detecting F− or TC, few studies have explored the use of a single type of CDs for the independent detection of both through multiple sensing mechanisms.

In this study, we introduce red-emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) capable of detecting fluoride (F−) and tetracycline (TC) via two distinct mechanisms (see Scheme 1 ). For F− detection, F− displace Fe3+ from the surface of R-CDs, restoring the fluorescence previously quenched by Fe3+. This competitive binding process forms the basis of a fluorescence-enhanced sensor with high selectivity and sensitivity. For TC detection, the significant spectral overlap between TC's absorption and the excitation spectrum of R-CDs leads to fluorescence quenching via a combination of static quenching and IFE. This dual-sensing approach enables the independent quantification of F− and TC in food matrices and water samples with satisfactory results. Our findings highlight the potential of R-CDs for practical applications in food safety and environmental monitoring. This study also presents a novel strategy for multi-analyte detection, demonstrating the versatility of carbon dots in advanced sensing technologies.

R-CDs were prepared via a one-step solvothermal method. In brief, p-PDA (0.03 g) and NiCl2•6H2O (0.006 g) were dissolved in ethanol (10 mL) and sonicated for 5 min. The resulting clear solution was transferred to a 50 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the deep purple solution was concentrated using a rotary evaporator to remove excess ethanol, then redispersed in a small amount of ultrapure water. The solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and dialyzed against ultrapure water (Mw cut-off: 1.0 kDa) for 24 h, with water being replaced every 6 h. The purified R-CDs were obtained via freeze-drying and reconstituted in ultrapure water for further use.

To evaluate the fluorescence quenching effect of Fe3+ on R-CDs, the sensor's selectivity was tested against fifteen cations (K+, Na+, Ag+, Ba2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Cr3+, and Fe3+), each at a concentration of 250 μM.

For the working solution, 500 μL of R-CDs (0.02 mg•mL−1 in ethanol), 400 μL of Tris-HCl buffer (0.1 M, pH 6.8), and varying volumes of Fe3+ standard solution (0.01 M) were added to a quartz test tube. The total volume was adjusted to 1.0 mL with ultrapure water. After incubation at room temperature for 1 min, fluorescence intensity was measured at excitation wavelengths of 285 nm and 505 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of F− by R-CDs-Fe3+-based fluorescence enhanced sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the fluorescence recovery effect of F − on the R-CDs-Fe3+ system, 500 μL of R-CDs solution (0.02 mg•mL−1 in ethanol), 400 μL of Tris-HCl buffer (0.1 M, pH 6.8), and 10 μL of Fe3+ standard solution (0.01 M) were mixed in a quartz test tube. After 1 min of thorough mixing, varying volumes of F− (0.01 M) were introduced, and the mixture was diluted to a final volume of 1.0 mL with ultrapure water. Fluorescence recovery was then recorded. To assess selectivity, other competing anions (1.0 mM) were also introduced into the system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TC using the R-CDs-based fluorescence quenching sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the selectivity of R-CDs toward TC, 15 amino acids (1–15: proline, alanine, leucine, glycine, aspartic acid, glutamic acid, valine, serine, lysine, arginine, isoleucine, cysteine, histidine, methionine, and threonine) and 12 antibiotics (16–27: penicillin, ornidazole, tinidazole, erythromycin, gentamicin, kanamycin, thiamphenicol, azithromycin, amoxicillin, tetracycline, oxytetracycline, and chlortetracycline) were prepared at a concentration of 0.01 M. Each compound (1.0 mM) was added to an R-CDs solution (0.01 mg•mL−1), and fluorescence intensity was recorded.

For TC detection, 500 μL of R-CDs solution (0.02 mg•mL−1 in ethanol), 400 μL of Tris-HCl buffer (0.1 M, pH 6.8), and varying volumes of TC solution (0.01 M) were mixed in a quartz test tube. Ultrapure water was added to a final volume of 1.0 mL, and fluorescence spectra were recorded at excitation wavelengths of 285 nm and 505 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of F− and TC in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Milk, honey, toothpaste, and tea samples were purchased from a local supermarket in Taiyuan, China. Sample preparation was performed following established protocols [25]. A 99 % (v/v) milk sample was blended with 1 % (v/v) trichloroacetic acid (300 g•L−1) and sonicated for 20 min to remove proteins and lipids. After centrifugation (5 min, 8000 rpm), the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane, and the pH was adjusted to 7.0 using 5.0 M NaOH. The honey sample was diluted 20-fold with ultrapure water and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. Toothpaste and tea samples were first soaked in a fluoride-containing aqueous solution and then immersed in ultrapure water to extract F − . The resulting supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane (see Scheme S1). Tap water, surface water, and mineral water were filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane before analysis. The standard addition method was employed to validate the sensor's practicality.

R-CDs were synthesized via a one-step solvothermal method using p-PDA and NiCl2•6H2O as precursors. Nickel ions acted as catalysts, enhancing the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of R-CDs (Table S1). Post-synthesis, organic solvents were removed via spin evaporation, and residual reactants were eliminated through dialysis (Fig. 1 a). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to characterize the morphology of R-CDs. The TEM image (Fig. 1b) reveals that R-CDs are uniformly distributed spheroidal nanoparticles with an average diameter of 2.25 nm. The high-resolution TEM inset in Fig. 1b shows a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite carbon, indicating the highly crystalline structure of the synthesized R-CDs [26].

The chemical composition of R-CDs was analyzed using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Fig. 1c). The presence of hydrophilic functional groups, including O–H/N–H (3080–3640 cm−1), C–N (1292 cm−1), and C–O (1132 cm−1), contributes to the high water solubility of R-CDs (≥ 1.0 mg•mL−1) [27,28]. Characteristic peaks at 1620 cm−1 and 1515 cm−1 correspond to CO/CN and CC stretching vibrations, respectively, indicating the presence of aromatic nuclei and conjugated systems [27]. Additionally, peaks observed at 2820–2990 cm−1 and 830 cm−1 are attributed to C–H stretching and out-of-plane C–H bending vibration, respectively, confirming the presence of an alkyl polymer shell [27,29]. To further investigate the surface composition, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed. The full XPS spectrum (Fig. 1d) reveals the presence of C, N, and O elements. The absence of a Ni peak in the 850–870 eV range, along with a Ni content of only 0.68 % measured by inductively coupled plasma (ICP) analysis (Table S2), suggests that nickel primarily serves as a catalyst rather than a dopant in R-CDs synthesis. High-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1e) displays three prominent peaks at 288.8 eV (CO), 286.2 eV (C–N/C–O), and 284.8 eV (CC/C–C/C–H) [30]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1f) shows peaks at 401.2 eV, 400.0 eV, and 398.6 eV, corresponding to graphitic N, pyrrolic N, and pyridinic N, respectively [27]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 1g) features peaks at 531.8 eV and 533.2 eV, assigned to CO and C–O, respectively [27]. These results suggest the presence of functional groups such as –OH, –COOH, –CONH2, and –NH2, which enhance the stability of R-CDs in aqueous solutions and facilitate their interactions with metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and stability of R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of R-CDs were characterized using ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption and fluorescence (FL) spectroscopy (Fig. 2 a). The UV–vis absorption spectrum (blue line) exhibits two absorption peaks at 250 nm and 280 nm, attributing to π→π∗ transitions in the aromatic system [28]. A broad peak at 520 nm is associated with n→π∗ transitions of C–O or C–N surface groups [24]. The FL spectra (red line) reveal an emission peak at 600 nm upon excitation at 285 nm, with a large Stokes shift of 315 nm, reducing autofluorescence interference in biological systems [20]. R-CDs exhibit two excitation wavelengths at 285 nm and 505 nm, both leading to an emission at 600 nm. Three-dimensional fluorescence spectra (Fig. 2b) demonstrate excitation-independent emission behavior, suggesting uniform surface states and a single dominant emission site [31]. The fluorescence QY of R-CDs, determined using rhodamine 6G as a reference (Fig. S1a), was approximately 12.26 % (Fig. S1b). Furthermore, fluorescence stability assessments in complex matrices (Figs. S2a–g) confirm the robustness of R-CDs, making them suitable for fluorescence sensing in diverse environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and optical mechanism of the fluorescence-enhanced sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Metal ions often quench the fluorescence of CDs via rapid electron transfer or strong coordination, forming the basis for fluorescence-enhanced sensors [32]. Selectivity and titration experiments (Figs. S3a–c) indicate that Fe3+ significantly quenches the fluorescence of R-CDs. Thus, 100 μM Fe3+ was selected for subsequent studies. To investigate the quenching mechanism, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), a strong metal-chelating ligand, was introduced into the R-CDs solution. The initially quenched fluorescence was rapidly restored (Fig. S3d), confirming that Fe3+ induces reversible luminescence changes in R-CDs. This reversibility underlies the use of R-CDs as a fluorescence sensor for Fe3+-chelating species such as F−. The detection scheme is illustrated in Fig. 3 a. In the absence of Fe3+, R-CDs exhibit strong fluorescence under excitation at 285 nm and 505 nm, representing the “on” state (Fig. 3b). Upon Fe3+ addition, fluorescence is quenched, transitioning the sensor to an “off” state (Fig. 3c). The subsequent introduction of F− restores fluorescence, returning the system to its “on” state (Fig. 3d), demonstrating the sensor's reversible fluorescence response to F−.

ce:section.ce:section-title: R-CDs-Fe3+-based fluorescence-enhanced sensor for F− detection
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the sensitivity of the R-CDs-Fe3+ complex as a fluorescence-enhanced sensor, we conducted quantitative detection of F−. A dispersion of R-CDs was treated with 100 μM Fe3+, and fluorescence emission spectra were recorded after a 1-min incubation with varying concentrations of F− (Fig. 3e). As F− was introduced into the R-CDs-Fe3+ system, fluorescence intensity was restored in a concentration-dependent manner (Fig. 3f). The fluorescence response (ΔF) exhibited a linear relationship with F− concentration in the range of 0.5–120 μM, following the equations: ΔF = 1.830CF − + 5.617 (R2 = 0.998, λex = 285 nm) and ΔF = 0.440CF − + 1.676 (R2 = 0.997, λex = 505 nm). Using the 3σ/slope method, the calculated limits of detection (LODs) for F− were 0.20 μM (λex = 285 nm) and 0.41 μM (λex = 505 nm). These values are significantly lower than the World Health Organization's permissible limit for F− in drinking water (1.0 mg•L−1, equal to 52.6 μM) [33]. Compared to other reported CDs-based F− sensors (Table S3), the R-CDs-Fe3+ system offers several advantages, including longer emission wavelengths, wider detection windows, and lower detection limits, highlighting its potential for efficient and sensitive F− detection. The selectivity and anti-interference capability of the R-CDs-Fe3+ sensor were further confirmed (Fig. 3g). The strong selectivity of Fe3+ for F− is attributed to the formation of a highly stable FeF3 complex, driven by the strong affinity between Fe3+ (a hard Lewis acid) and F− (a hard Lewis base). In contrast, other anions, such as Cl−, form weaker complexes (e.g., FeCl3) under the same conditions, resulting in negligible interference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching and recovery mechanism for F− detection
ce:section.ce:para: To gain deeper insight into the fluorescence quenching and recovery dynamics, we evaluated the binding interactions between Fe3+ and F− (see Supplementary Material). The binding constant of the R-CDs-Fe3+ complex was calculated to be 6.9 × 103 L•mol−1, based on the ratio of the intercept to the slope of the linear equation (Fig. 4 a). This value is significantly lower than that of FeF3 (14.5 × 103 L•mol−1, Fig. S4), indicating that Fe3+ preferentially binds to F− over R-CDs. Consequently, Fe3+ dissociates from the R-CDs-Fe3+ complex, restoring the surface functional groups of R-CDs and leading to an immediate recovery of fluorescence intensity [32,34]. A schematic illustration of this interaction is provided in Fig. 4b.

To further investigate the structural changes underlying fluorescence quenching, we examined the morphology of R-CDs after Fe3+ addition using TEM. The HR-TEM image (Fig. 4c) reveals significant aggregation of R-CDs, likely due to interactions between Fe3+ and the carboxyl or electron-donating nitrogen-containing groups on R-CDs, consistent with previous reports [34,35]. This aggregation explains the fluorescence quenching effect induced by Fe3+. However, upon introducing F−, the R-CDs depolymerize, and needle-shaped crystals emerge (Fig. 4d), indicating the formation of stable (FeFx)n− complexes with Fe3+ [36]. As a result, Fe3+ is effectively removed from the R-CDs surface, restoring fluorescence.

To further validate this mechanism, FTIR and XPS analyses were conducted to examine the interactions between R-CDs and Fe3+. In the FTIR spectra (Fig. S5), the characteristic peaks at 3200 cm−1 (amino N) [37] and 1292 cm−1 (C–N) disappear upon Fe3+ binding but reappear after F− addition, confirming that fluorescence quenching is associated with Fe3+ coordination to amino N and C–N groups. XPS analysis provided additional insights. The C 1s spectrum revealed a shift in the C–N/C–O bond signal from 286.2 eV (Fig. 1e) to 285.6 eV (Fig. 5 a) and in the CO bond signal from 288.8 eV to 289.3 eV upon Fe3+ binding. After adding F−, these C 1s signals reverted to 286.0 eV and 288.8 eV, respectively (Fig. 5b), further supporting the hypothesis that fluorescence quenching is mediated by Fe3+ interactions with C–N/C–O and CO groups. Similarly, the N 1s XPS spectra (Fig. 5c) exhibited shifts of 0.4 eV in pyrrolic N and 1.2 eV in graphitic N, along with the complete disappearance of pyridinic N at 398.6 eV after Fe3+ binding. This suggests a reduction in electron density due to Fe3+ coordination [38]. Upon F− addition, these N 1s signals (Fig. 5d) returned to their original positions, indicating restoration of the electron density in nitrogen-containing functional groups [28]. In the O 1s spectra, the CO peak shifted by 0.7 eV, and the C–O signal weakened upon Fe3+ binding (Fig. 5e), indicating coordination between Fe3+ and carboxyl groups. After introducing F−, the CO peak returned to 531.8 eV, and the C–O intensity increased (Fig. 5f), signifying the disruption of Fe3+-carboxyl coordination. This restoration of nitrogen and oxygen functional groups correlates with the fluorescence recovery of R-CDs.

The fluorescence quenching and recovery mechanism was further elucidated using the F 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 5g). Upon fluorescence restoration, the freeze-dried sample exhibited two distinct F 1s signals at 685.6 eV and 684.8 eV, attributed to the formation of FeF3 complexes [39] and N–Fe–F bonds [35], respectively. The presence of FeF3 strongly supports the proposed recovery mechanism, while the presence of N–Fe–F bonds indicates that a portion of the adsorbed Fe3+ remains attached to the R-CDs surface. Further insights were gained from the Fe 2p XPS spectra before and after F− introduction (Fig. 5h and i), where all Fe 2p signals displayed a negative shift, confirming an interaction between Fe3+ and F−. The Fe 2p XPS spectrum after F− addition (Fig. 5i) further substantiated the formation of FeF3 while indicating that some Fe3+ remained bound to nitrogen-containing functional groups [35,39]. This evidence supports a competitive binding effect, where F− displaces Fe3+ from carboxyl and nitrogen groups on the R-CDs surface, thereby reversing the Fe3+-induced fluorescence quenching and restoring the emission.

To assess the practical applicability of the R-CDs-Fe3+ sensor for F− detection, standard addition tests and fluorine reagent spectrophotometry (GB, HJ 488–2009) [40] were conducted. As summarized in Table 1 and Table S4, spiked recoveries in water and food samples ranged from 97.1 % to 103.5 %, with relative standard deviations (RSDs) between 1.18 % and 3.25 %. Additionally, Table 1 demonstrates a strong agreement between results obtained using the standard method and those from our proposed fluorescence sensor. These findings confirm the high reliability and accuracy of the R-CDs-Fe3+ fluorescence-enhanced sensor for quantitative F− detection in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching fluorescence sensor for TC detection
Fluorescence response of R-CDs to TC
ce:section.ce:para: Under excitation at 285 nm, the fluorescence of R-CDs was significantly quenched by TC. However, under 505 nm excitation, the fluorescence showed only a slight decrease (ΔF/F0 = 38.6 %), whereas the quenching efficiency was significantly higher at 285 nm (ΔF/F0 = 91.2 %) (Fig. 6 a). This differential response enabled the 285 nm excitation fluorescence to serve as a responsive signal for TC detection, while the 505 nm excitation fluorescence functioned as a reference to minimize background interference. As shown in Fig. 6b, the ratio of fluorescence intensities (Fλex=285 nm/Fλex=505 nm) exhibited a linear correlation with TC concentration over the range of 0.5–130 μM, following the equation: Fλex=285 nm/Fλex=505 nm = −0.017CTC + 3.644 (R2 = 0.997). The LOD was determined to be 0.16 μM (3σ/K).

Compared to other reported CDs for TC detection (Table S5), our synthesized R-CDs exhibit a longer emission wavelength, a wider linear range, and enhanced detection reliability due to the ratiometric sensing strategy. Simultaneously, the R-CDs sensor demonstrated good selectivity and anti-interference capability, as illustrated in Fig. 6c.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching mechanism of R-CDs by TC
ce:section.ce:para: To elucidate the quenching mechanism, various potential interactions between R-CDs and TC were examined. Common quenching pathways in CDs-based include PET, FRET, IFE, dynamic quenching, and static quenching [18]. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) curve (Fig. 6d) revealed an initial reduction potential of −0.40 eV for R-CDs, corresponding to a LUMO energy of −4.00 eV. The energy band gap (Eg) was determined as 4.08 eV (Fig. 6e), yielding a calculated HOMO energy of −8.08 eV. For TC, LUMO and HOMO energies were determined as −3.87 eV and −6.20 eV, respectively [23]. Since the photo-excited electrons in R-CDs cannot transfer to the LUMO of TC, PET was ruled out as a potential quenching mechanism (Fig. 6f). No significant spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of R-CDs and the absorption spectrum of TC indicates that FRET does not contribute to fluorescence quenching (Fig. S6).

The absorption spectrum of TC shows significant overlap with the excitation spectrum of R-CDs in the short-wavelength region (285 nm) (Fig. 6g, red shadow), but only slight overlap in the long-wavelength region (505 nm, Fig. 6g, blue shadow). This difference explains why fluorescence quenching was more pronounced at 285 nm. To further verify the involvement of IFE, corrected fluorescence (CF) results and inhibition efficiency (E) values at different TC concentrations were analyzed (Table S6 and Fig. 6h). The inhibition efficiency initially increased with TC concentration and then stabilized, suggesting that IFE contributes to approximately half of the total quenching effect.

The fluorescence lifetime of R-CDs remained nearly unchanged upon TC addition (Fig. 6i and Table S7), ruling out dynamic quenching and suggesting the presence of static quenching (SQE). The Stern-Volmer equation was applied to analyze quenching behavior, revealing a decreasing quenching constant (K
SV ) with increasing temperature (Table S8). Additionally, the calculated diffusion-controlled quenching rate constant (k
q ) was 1.61 × 1012 M−1 s−1 (K
SV = 1.00 × 104 M−1, τ0 = 6.20 ns), exceeding the diffusion-controlled limit of 1.0 × 1010 M−1 s−1, further confirming SQE rather than dynamic quenching (Fig. 6j). SQE was further supported by UV–vis spectral changes (Fig. S7), which revealed an increase in absorption intensity and a peak shift from 282 nm to 270 nm with rising TC concentration. This suggests the formation of a ground-state complex between R-CDs and TC, a hallmark of SQE. The association constant (K
LB ) for SQE was calculated using the Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal equation (Fig. 6k and Table S8). The results display that K
LB decreases with increasing temperature, which indicates the gradual dissociation of the ground-state complex and reduced stability of the complex at higher temperatures. This supports the conclusion that fluorescence quenching is primarily due to SQE. Molecular docking simulations (Fig. S8) confirmed favorable binding between R-CDs and TC, with a binding energy of −5.6 kcal/mol (Table S9). The docking pose (Fig. S8c) revealed multiple hydrogen bonds between TC's hydroxyl/carbonyl groups and R-CDs surface functional groups, stabilizing the complex and reinforcing the SQE mechanism.

The fluorescence quenching of R-CDs by TC arises from a synergistic effect of IFE and SQE (Fig. 6l). The combination of spectroscopic analysis, fluorescence lifetime measurements, and molecular docking strongly supports this dual mechanism, confirming the effectiveness of R-CDs as a sensitive and selective ratiometric sensor for TC detection.

Having demonstrated the excellent selectivity of the R-CDs sensor for TC, we further evaluated its applicability in real sample analysis using the standard addition method. As summarized in Table 2 , the recoveries in water and food samples ranged from 96.1 % to 105.3 %, with RSDs below 6.0 %. These results confirm the excellent reliability and accuracy of the constructed fluorescence-quenching sensor for TC detection in complex sample matrices.

In this study, we synthesized red-emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) with a significant Stokes shift, enabling the sensitive detection of F− and TC in food and water samples. The F− sensing mechanism relies on the displacement of Fe3+, which is weakly bound to nitrogen and carboxyl groups on the R-CDs surface, causing fluorescence quenching. Upon F− introduction, Fe3+ is competitively displaced, restoring the fluorescence signal. This fluorescence-enhanced sensor exhibited a detection limit of 0.20 μM for F− and demonstrated excellent performance in real samples analysis, with recoveries ranging from 97.1 % to 103.5 %. For TC detection, fluorescence quenching was attributed to a synergistic effect of IFE and SQE. The addition of TC led to a gradual fluorescence decrease, yielding a detection limit of 0.16 μM, with recoveries between 96.1 % and 105.3 %. This dual-analyte sensing approach, utilizing a single carbon dot system with distinct detection mechanisms, expands the practical applicability of CDs-based sensors. By integrating rational sensor design principles, this method offers a more reliable, selective, and theoretically grounded strategy for multi-target detection, avoiding arbitrary target selection. The developed system presents a versatile and robust sensing platform for environmental and biological analyses.

Yumin Hao: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xiaole Wen: Software. Xiaofang Zheng: Formal analysis. Huiping Wang: Funding acquisition. Wenjuan Dong: Validation. Yang Liu: Methodology. Shengmei Song: Software. Shaomin Shuang: Writing – review & editing. Yujing Guo: Validation, Supervision. Chuan Dong: Supervision, Project administration. Xiaojuan Gong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Central Guidance Fund for Science and Technology Development (YDZJSX2021A004 and YDZJSX2022C002), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21705101 and 22274090), and Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province (202403021212049).
